questions.question: How do Custom Agent permissions function in relation to individual user permissions?

options.text: They are identical to the permissions of the user who created them.
options.explanation: Custom Agents operate with their own independent permissions separate from individual user permissions.

options.text: They are completely restricted to only allow read-only access.
options.explanation: Agents have independent permissions, meaning they might access information that individual users could not access directly.

options.text: They are independent of individual user permissions.
options.explanation: Agents operate with their own independent permissions separate from individual user permissions.

options.text: They are inherited from the workspace admin's permissions.
options.explanation: Agents do not inherit permissions; they have their own independent permissions.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a tool available to Workspace admins for managing Custom Agents?

options.text: Agent Directory
options.explanation: Admins can use an Agent Directory to maintain visibility over all agents.

options.text: Content Search
options.explanation: Content search is listed as a tool for managing agents across the organization.

options.text: Employee Directory
options.explanation: While admins have access to analytics and audit logs, an 'Employee Directory' is not mentioned as a management tool for agents.

options.text: Ownership transfer tools
options.explanation: Ownership transfer tools are listed as a method for admins to manage agents.

questions.question: Which of the following resources does NOT trigger a warning when sharing a Custom Agent?

options.text: Third-party integrations
options.explanation: Warnings include third-party integrations, private Notion pages, email/calendar data, and access to all pages shared with everyone.

options.text: Access to all pages that are shared with everyone in the workspace
options.explanation: Access to all pages shared with everyone triggers a warning to ensure the user considers who is accessing the agent.

options.text: Public Notion pages
options.explanation: The text specifies warnings for private pages, email/calendar data, and third-party integrations. Public pages are not listed as a trigger.

options.text: Email or Calendar data
options.explanation: Warnings specifically include email or calendar data to prevent unintended data sharing.

questions.question: Who is authorized to add more Slack channels or increase access (e.g., from Read Only to Read & Reply) for a Custom Agent connected to Slack?

options.text: Any user with 'Can Edit' permissions
options.explanation: While users with 'Can Edit' can remove connections, they cannot expand what the connection can see.

options.text: Only the person who connected the integration
options.explanation: For each Custom Agent, only the person who connected an integration can add more Slack channels or increase access.

options.text: Only workspace admins
options.explanation: While admins have broad control, the specific restriction for adding channels belongs to the connector.

options.text: Only users with 'Can view and interact' permissions
options.explanation: View-only users cannot modify connection settings.

questions.question: How does the 'Automatic editor access checks' feature protect sensitive data?

options.text: It automatically deletes the agent if permissions change.
options.explanation: The agent does not automatically delete; it stops running instead.

options.text: It requires re-approval from all editors whenever a resource is accessed.
options.explanation: It checks permissions at the start, but does not require re-approval on every access.

options.text: It stops the agent from running if all editors lose access.
options.explanation: If all editors lose access to a resource, the agent stops running, preventing 'orphaned' agents from continuing to access sensitive data.

options.text: It restricts the agent to only private pages.
options.explanation: The feature prevents access loss, it does not restrict the agent's scope to private pages only.

questions.question: Where do Custom Agents appear in the interface regarding page access?

options.text: Only in the AI sidebar
options.explanation: Agents appear in the share menu of any page or database they have access to.

options.text: In the share menu of any page or database they have access to, just like people
options.explanation: Agents appear in the share menu of any page or database they have access to, just like people.

options.text: Nowhere, they are managed separately from pages
options.explanation: Agents are visible in the share menu of pages and databases they access.

options.text: Only in the Agent Directory
options.explanation: While the Directory lists them, they also appear in the share menu of specific pages.

questions.question: A user with 'Can Edit' access wants to change the Slack channel a Custom Agent can access. What must they do?

options.text: They can edit it directly by changing the channel settings.
options.explanation: Users with 'Can Edit' access can remove connections but cannot expand what the connection can see.

options.text: They must remove and then reconnect the integration.
options.explanation: To edit third-party connection settings an editor did not set up, they must remove and then reconnect the integration.

options.text: They must ask a workspace admin to make the change.
options.explanation: The user has 'Can Edit' access, which allows them to remove and reconnect integrations.

options.text: They can only view the current channels and cannot change them.
options.explanation: They can remove the connection, but to add new ones, they must reconnect.

questions.question: Which permission level allows a user to see the full settings page of a Custom Agent in view-only mode?

options.text: Full Access
options.explanation: Full Access allows editing and removing agents, but the text specifies that 'Can view and interact' is sufficient for viewing settings.

options.text: Can view and interact
options.explanation: Users with 'Can view and interact' permissions can see the agent's full settings page in view-only mode (instructions, pages, tools, config) but cannot edit, share, or modify.

options.text: Can view
options.explanation: The text specifically mentions 'Can view and interact' as the permission for viewing settings.

options.text: Can edit
options.explanation: Can edit permissions allow editing and removing, but the text highlights the view-only capability of 'Can view and interact'.

questions.question: What action does Custom Agents take regarding unexpected external links?

options.text: The agent automatically follows the link to check if it is safe.
options.explanation: The agent pauses before accessing the link.

options.text: The agent ignores the link entirely.
options.explanation: The agent does not ignore the link; it pauses to request confirmation.

options.text: The agent pauses and asks for confirmation.
options.explanation: If the agent generates a URL not in the original prompt, it pauses and asks for confirmation before proceeding.

options.text: The link is blocked immediately by the platform.
options.explanation: The agent pauses and asks the user to review and approve the URL.

questions.question: When working with sensitive data or content from external or untrusted sources, which model type is recommended to minimize the risk of prompt injection?

options.text: Smaller models
options.explanation: Smaller models are generally faster and cheaper but are 'more vulnerable to prompt injection'.

options.text: Larger models
options.explanation: Larger models perform better on complex tasks, including distinguishing legitimate instructions from malicious ones.

options.text: Random models
options.explanation: Model selection is deliberate based on risk factors, not random.

options.text: No models (disable AI)
options.explanation: The text advises selecting a larger model to handle untrusted content safely.

questions.question: Which of the following is a reason to select a larger AI model?

options.text: To save money on processing costs
options.explanation: While smaller models are cheaper, the recommendation for sensitive data focuses on performance and security over cost.

options.text: To perform faster on simple text translation
options.explanation: Smaller models are generally faster for simple tasks.

options.text: To better distinguish legitimate instructions from malicious ones
options.explanation: Larger models have more parameters, making them better at complex tasks like distinguishing legitimate instructions from malicious ones.

options.text: To process uploaded files from trusted sources
options.explanation: The text suggests larger models for sensitive data or content from external/untrusted sources.

questions.question: Who can remove a Custom Agent from a page or database?

options.text: Only the person who created the agent
options.explanation: Full access to the page is required, not just creation rights.

options.text: Anyone with Full Access to the page or database
options.explanation: If you have Full Access to the page or database, you'll see their permission level and can remove them if needed.

options.text: Only workspace admins
options.explanation: Full access users, not just admins, can remove agents.

options.text: Only users with 'Can view and interact' permissions
options.explanation: View and interact users can view settings but cannot remove agents.
